title: University of Southern California

infobox.value: "Let whoever earns the palm bear it"
[WSCUC](/wiki/Western_Association_of_Schools_and_Colleges_-_Senior_College_and_University_Commission "Western Association of Schools and Colleges - Senior College and University Commission")

* [AAU](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") * [APRU](/wiki/Association_of_Pacific_Rim_Universities "Association of Pacific Rim Universities") * [NAICU](/wiki/National_Association_of_Independent_Colleges_and_Universities "National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities")[[1]](#cite_note-1) * [UARC](/wiki/University_Affiliated_Research_Center "University Affiliated Research Center") * [Sea-grant](/wiki/National_Sea_Grant_College_Program "National Sea Grant College Program") * [Space-grant](/wiki/National_Space_Grant_College_and_Fellowship_Program "National Space Grant College and Fellowship Program")
University Park campus, 226 acres (0.91 km2)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Health Sciences campus, 79 acres (0.32 km2)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

* [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [FBS](/wiki/Football_Bowl_Subdivision "Football Bowl Subdivision") – [Big Ten](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") * [ACHA](/wiki/American_Collegiate_Hockey_Association "American Collegiate Hockey Association") * [MPSF](/wiki/Mountain_Pacific_Sports_Federation "Mountain Pacific Sports Federation")

(**USC**, **SC**, or **Southern Cal**[[a]](#endnote_Anone)) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California, United States. Founded in 1880 by [Robert M. Widney](/wiki/Robert_M._Widney "Robert M. Widney"), it is the oldest private research university in California[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) and has an enrollment of more than 47,000 students.[[4]](#cite_note-Common_Data_Set-4)

The **University of Southern California** (**USC**, **SC**, or **Southern Cal**[[a]](#endnote_Anone)) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California, United States. Founded in 1880 by [Robert M. Widney](/wiki/Robert_M._Widney "Robert M. Widney"), it is the oldest private research university in California[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) and has an enrollment of more than 47,000 students.[[4]](#cite_note-Common_Data_Set-4)

The university is composed of a [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts_education "Liberal arts education") school, the [Dornsife College of Letters, Arts and Sciences](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California_academics "University of Southern California academics"), and 22 [undergraduate](/wiki/Undergraduate_education "Undergraduate education"), [graduate](/wiki/Graduate_school "Graduate school"), and professional schools, enrolling roughly 21,000 undergraduate and 28,500 [post-graduate](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education") students from all fifty [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") and more than 115 countries.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) It is a member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities"), which it joined in 1969.

USC sponsors a variety of intercollegiate sports and competes in the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) and the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Members of USC's sports teams, the [Trojans](/wiki/USC_Trojans "USC Trojans"), have won 107 NCAA team championships and 412 NCAA individual championships.[[17]](#cite_note-ncaa-championship-17) As of 2021, Trojan athletes have won 326 medals at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") ([153 golds, 96 silvers, and 77 bronzes](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medals "List of American universities with Olympic medals")), more than any other American university.[[18]](#cite_note-:3-18) USC has had 571 football players drafted to the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), the second-highest number of draftees in the country.[[19]](#cite_note-nfldraft-19)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the University of Southern California](/wiki/History_of_the_University_of_Southern_California "History of the University of Southern California")

### Founding and early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding and early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Robert_Maclay_Widney_ca1885.jpg/250px-Robert_Maclay_Widney_ca1885.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Maclay_Widney_ca1885.jpg)

[Robert Maclay Widney](/wiki/Robert_Maclay_Widney "Robert Maclay Widney"), founder of the university, photographed in 1885

The University of Southern California was founded following the efforts of Judge [Robert Maclay Widney](/wiki/Robert_Maclay_Widney "Robert Maclay Widney"), who helped secure donations from several key figures in early Los Angeles history: a Protestant nurseryman, [Ozro Childs](/wiki/Ozro_Childs "Ozro Childs"); an [Irish Catholic](/wiki/Irish_Catholic "Irish Catholic") former governor, [John Gately Downey](/wiki/John_Gately_Downey "John Gately Downey"); and a German Jewish banker, [Isaias Wolf Hellman](/wiki/Isaias_W._Hellman "Isaias W. Hellman"). The three donated 308 acres to establish the campus and provided the necessary seed money for the construction of the first buildings. Originally operated in affiliation with the Methodist Church, the school mandated from the start that "no student would be denied admission because of race". The university is no longer affiliated with any church, having severed formal ties in 1952. When USC opened in 1880, the school had an enrollment of 53 students and a faculty of 10. Its first graduating class in 1884 was a class of three: two men and one woman, who was [valedictorian](/wiki/Valedictorian "Valedictorian").

USC was further expanded with the construction of Old College in 1887, which served as the college of [Liberal Arts](/wiki/Liberal_Arts "Liberal Arts") (now Dornsife College of Letters, Arts, and Sciences). Although envisioned as the permanent centerpiece to the university, its age and lack of earthquake safety became major concerns as USC moved through the 20th century.[[20]](#cite_note-hmdb.org-20) The building was eventually demolished in 1948.

### USC in the 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: USC in the 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Widney_1903.jpg/250px-Widney_1903.jpg)](/wiki/File:Widney_1903.jpg)

The Widney Alumni House, the campus's first building, photographed in 1903

Despite a growing student and faculty population, the university maintained most of its campus along Trousdale Parkway (then known as University Avenue). Beginning in 1919, architect John Parkinson developed a master plan for the university that expanded beyond this avenue, focusing on Romanesque buildings for which the university has become notable.[[20]](#cite_note-hmdb.org-20) Bovard Administration Building was completed in 1921 along this parkway, and remains one of the university's oldest and most iconic buildings. The Gwynn Wilson Student Union, [Doheny Memorial Library](/wiki/Doheny_Memorial_Library "Doheny Memorial Library"), and [Allan Hancock](/wiki/Allan_Hancock "Allan Hancock") Foundation were completed in 1927, 1932, and 1940, respectively, and remain directly adjacent to the university's central quad (now Hahn Plaza).[[21]](#cite_note-21) Many of these were constructed under President [Rufus B. von KleinSmid](/wiki/Rufus_B._von_KleinSmid "Rufus B. von KleinSmid"), who spearheaded the creation of 19 buildings over 25 years.[[20]](#cite_note-hmdb.org-20)

The onset of World War II led to a transformation of campus life, with a shift in academic programs and student enrollment. Total enrollment fell 15%; military programs were instituted, and by the end of the war 75% of male students were involved in some branch of the military. USC was one of 131 colleges and universities nationally that took part in the [V-12 Navy College Training Program](/wiki/V-12_Navy_College_Training_Program "V-12 Navy College Training Program"), which offered students a path to a Navy commission. After the war ended, enrollment of veterans under the [G.I. Bill](/wiki/G.I._Bill "G.I. Bill") soared to 24,000 by 1947, straining USC's facilities and resources.[[22]](#cite_note-usc-v-12-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 1950, Founder's Hall (now Taper Hall of Humanities) was constructed, becoming the first new classroom space in a decade. University Avenue was closed to through traffic. In 1958, [Norman Topping](/wiki/Norman_Topping "Norman Topping") became president, and initiated a campaign for capital construction to support USC's burgeoning population. Under the assistance of architect [William Pereira](/wiki/William_Pereira "William Pereira"), USC constructed 99 buildings between 1961 and 1979.[[20]](#cite_note-hmdb.org-20) The university hosted some Olympics activities in 1984, and in 1990, [Steven Sample](/wiki/Steven_Sample "Steven Sample") became president of the university.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Origin of USC's mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Origin of USC's mascot")]

USC students and athletes are known as Trojans, epitomized by the [Trojan Shrine](/wiki/Trojan_Shrine "Trojan Shrine"), nicknamed "Tommy Trojan", near the center of campus. Until 1912, USC students (especially athletes) were known as Fighting Methodists or Wesleyans, though neither name was approved by the university. Tommy's sword has been stolen so frequently that instead of replacing it with an expensive brass one each time, he is now provided with a wooden one.[[25]](#cite_note-25) During a fateful track and field meet with [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), the USC team was beaten early and seemingly conclusively. After only the first few events, it seemed implausible USC would ever win, but the team fought back, winning many of the later events, to lose only by a slight margin. After this contest, *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* sportswriter Owen Bird reported the USC athletes "fought on like the [Trojans](/wiki/Trojan_War "Trojan War") of antiquity", and the president of the university at the time, [George F. Bovard](/wiki/George_F._Bovard "George F. Bovard"), approved the name officially.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Campus")]

Main article: [Campus of the University of Southern California](/wiki/Campus_of_the_University_of_Southern_California "Campus of the University of Southern California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Doheny_Memorial_Library_of_USC.jpg/250px-Doheny_Memorial_Library_of_USC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doheny_Memorial_Library_of_USC.jpg)

The [Doheny Library](/wiki/Doheny_Library "Doheny Library")

The main campus is in the [University Park](/wiki/University_Park,_Los_Angeles "University Park, Los Angeles") neighborhood of Los Angeles, 2 miles (3.2 km) southwest of [downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles"). Located off exit 20B of [Interstate 110](/wiki/Interstate_110_(California) "Interstate 110 (California)"), the campus's boundaries are Jefferson Boulevard on the north and northeast, Figueroa Street on the southeast, Exposition Boulevard on the south, and Vermont Avenue on the west. Since the 1960s, through-campus vehicle traffic has been either severely restricted or entirely prohibited on some thoroughfares. The University Park campus is within walking distance to Los Angeles landmarks such as the [Shrine Auditorium](/wiki/Shrine_Auditorium "Shrine Auditorium") and [Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum"), which is operated and managed by the university.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Most buildings are in the [Romanesque Revival](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival_architecture "Romanesque Revival architecture") style, although some dormitories, engineering buildings, and physical sciences labs are of various [Modernist](/wiki/Modernist_architecture "Modernist architecture") styles (especially two large [Brutalist](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture") dormitories at the campus's northern edge) that sharply contrast with the predominantly red-brick campus. Widney Alumni House, built in 1880, is the oldest university building in Southern California. The historic portion of the main campus was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2015.

Besides its main campus at University Park, USC also operates the Health Sciences Campus about 2 miles (3.2 km) northeast of downtown. In addition, the Children's Hospital Los Angeles is staffed by USC faculty from the [Keck School of Medicine](/wiki/Keck_School_of_Medicine "Keck School of Medicine"), and is often referred to as USC's third campus. USC also operates an [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California") center in [Irvine](/wiki/Irvine,_California "Irvine, California") for business, pharmacy, social work, and education, and the [Information Sciences Institute](/wiki/Information_Sciences_Institute "Information Sciences Institute"), with centers in [Arlington, Virginia](/wiki/Arlington_County,_Virginia "Arlington County, Virginia"), and [Marina del Rey](/wiki/Marina_del_Rey,_California "Marina del Rey, California"). For its science students, USC operates the [Wrigley Institute for Environmental Studies](/wiki/USC_Wrigley_Institute_for_Environmental_Studies "USC Wrigley Institute for Environmental Studies") on [Catalina Island](/wiki/Santa_Catalina_Island,_California "Santa Catalina Island, California") just 20 miles (32 km) off the coast of Los Angeles, and home to the [Philip K. Wrigley](/wiki/Philip_K._Wrigley "Philip K. Wrigley") Marine Science Center.

The Price School of Public Policy also runs a satellite campus in [Sacramento](/wiki/Sacramento,_California "Sacramento, California"). A Health Sciences [Alhambra](/wiki/Alhambra,_California "Alhambra, California") campus holds the Primary Care Physician Assistant Program, the Institute for Health Promotion and Disease Prevention Research (IPR), and the Masters in Public Health Program. In 2005, USC established a federal relations office in Washington, DC., and in March 2023, USC announced the opening of a new Capital Campus in Washington, D.C. The university purchased a seven-story 60,000 square feet building and remodeled it to house classrooms, event venues, office spaces, a bookstore and a theater. Located in the heart of the Dupont Circle neighborhood, the USC Capital Campus is also home to USC's Office of Research Advancement, which helps university faculty researchers secure federal funding for multidisciplinary research projects. USC was developed under two master plans drafted and implemented some forty years apart. The first was prepared by [Train & Williams](/wiki/Train_%26_Williams "Train & Williams") but it was replaced by the second, made [the Parkinsons](/wiki/The_Parkinsons "The Parkinsons") in 1919.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/USC_VKC_building.jpg/250px-USC_VKC_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:USC_VKC_building.jpg)

The Center for International and Public Affairs, topped by a 5,500 lb (2,500 kg) globe, is the tallest structure on campus.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Built under the second master plan, it reflects a trend towards modernism during that period.

For its role in making visible and sustained improvements in the neighborhoods surrounding both the University Park and Health Sciences campuses, *Time/Princeton Review College Guide* named it College of the Year 2000. Roughly half of the university's students volunteer in community-service programs in neighborhoods around campus and throughout Los Angeles. These outreach programs, as well as previous administrations' commitment to remaining in [South Los Angeles](/wiki/South_Los_Angeles "South Los Angeles") amid widespread calls to move the campus following the 1965 [Watts Riots](/wiki/Watts_Riots "Watts Riots"), are credited for the safety of the university during the [1992 Los Angeles Riots](/wiki/1992_Los_Angeles_Riots "1992 Los Angeles Riots"). The [ZIP Code](/wiki/ZIP_Code "ZIP Code") for USC is 90089 and that of the surrounding University Park community is 90007.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/USC_globe_tower_and_Waite_Phillips_Hall.jpg/250px-USC_globe_tower_and_Waite_Phillips_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:USC_globe_tower_and_Waite_Phillips_Hall.jpg)

The globe tower and Waite Phillips Hall, home to the USC Rossier School of Education



Traveler horse statue at University of Southern California in Los Angeles

USC has an endowment of $8.1 billion and carries out nearly $1 billion per year in sponsored research.[[3]](#cite_note-About_USC-3)

### University Village

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: University Village")]

In 1999, USC purchased the University Park shopping center, which was demolished in 2014. In September of the same year, the university began construction on USC Village, a 1.25-million-square-foot residential and retail center directly adjacent to USC's University Park campus on 15 acres of land owned by the university.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The USC Village has over 130,000 square feet of retail space on the ground floor, with student housing on the four floors above. The $700 million project is the biggest development in the history of USC and is also one of the largest in the history of South Los Angeles. With a grand opening held on August 17, 2017,[[30]](#cite_note-30) the USC Village includes a Trader Joe's, a Target, a fitness center, restaurants, outdoor dining, 400 retail parking spots, a community room, and housing for 2,700 students.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Health Sciences campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Health Sciences campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Old_building_of_main_hospital%2C_Los_Angeles_County%2BUSC_Medical_Center_in_2014.jpg/250px-Old_building_of_main_hospital%2C_Los_Angeles_County%2BUSC_Medical_Center_in_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_building_of_main_hospital,_Los_Angeles_County%2BUSC_Medical_Center_in_2014.jpg)

The original Los Angeles County - USC Medical Center

Located three miles (5 km) from downtown Los Angeles and seven miles (11 km) from the [University Park](/wiki/University_Park,_Los_Angeles "University Park, Los Angeles") campus, USC's Health Sciences campus is a major center for basic and clinical biomedical research in the fields of [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), [gene therapy](/wiki/Gene_therapy "Gene therapy"), the [neurosciences](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience"), and [transplantation biology](/wiki/Organ_transplant "Organ transplant"), among others. The 79-acre (32 ha)[[32]](#cite_note-32) campus is home to the region's first and oldest medical and pharmacy schools.

In addition to the [Los Angeles County+USC Medical Center](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County%2BUSC_Medical_Center "Los Angeles County+USC Medical Center"), which is one of the nation's largest teaching hospitals, the campus includes three patient care facilities: [USC Norris Comprehensive Cancer Center](/wiki/USC_Norris_Comprehensive_Cancer_Center "USC Norris Comprehensive Cancer Center"), [Keck Hospital of USC](/wiki/Keck_Hospital_of_USC "Keck Hospital of USC"), and the USC Eye Institute. USC faculty staffs these and many other hospitals in Southern California, including the internationally acclaimed [Children's Hospital Los Angeles](/wiki/Children%27s_Hospital_Los_Angeles "Children's Hospital Los Angeles"). The health sciences campus is also home to the [USC School of Pharmacy](/wiki/USC_School_of_Pharmacy "USC School of Pharmacy") and several research buildings such as USC/Norris Cancer Research Tower, Institute for Genetic Medicine, [Zilkha Neurogenetic Institute](/wiki/Zilkha_Neurogenetic_Institute "Zilkha Neurogenetic Institute"), Harlyne J. Norris Cancer Research Tower and Eli and Edythe Broad Center for Regenerative Medicine and Stem Cell Research.

In July 2013, the university expanded its medical services into the foothill communities of northern Los Angeles when it acquired the 185 bed Verdugo Hills Hospital in [Glendale, California](/wiki/Glendale,_California "Glendale, California"). USC planned on making at least $30 million in capital improvements to the facility, which was officially renamed USC Verdugo Hills Hospital. This 40-year-old hospital provides the community a 24-hour emergency department, primary stroke center, maternity/labor and delivery, cardiac rehabilitation, and imaging and diagnostic services.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In July 2022, the university acquired the 348 bed Methodist Hospital of Southern California in [Arcadia, California](/wiki/Arcadia,_California "Arcadia, California").[[34]](#cite_note-34) Renamed USC Arcadia Hospital it is a full-service community hospital offering advanced cardiovascular services including cardiac catheterization, electrophysiology and open-heart surgery. [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County "Los Angeles County") has designated it as both a heart attack receiving center and a comprehensive stroke center, as well as an Emergency Department Approved for Pediatrics. The hospital also offers a variety of surgical services in orthopaedics, neurosurgery, obstetrics, gynecology, and cancer care, plus physical rehabilitation and many other medical specialties.[[35]](#cite_note-35) USC physicians serve more than one million patients each year.

### Public transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Public transit")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Mudd_Hall_Expo_Park_USC_Los_Angeles_metro_stop_2019.jpg/250px-Mudd_Hall_Expo_Park_USC_Los_Angeles_metro_stop_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mudd_Hall_Expo_Park_USC_Los_Angeles_metro_stop_2019.jpg)

[Expo Park/USC station](/wiki/Expo_Park/USC_station "Expo Park/USC station") and Mudd Hall of Philosophy in the background

USC is served by several rapid transit stations. The [Metro E Line](/wiki/E_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "E Line (Los Angeles Metro)") [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") service between [Downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles") and [Santa Monica](/wiki/Santa_Monica "Santa Monica") wraps around the south and eastern edges of the University Park campus. The E Line has three stations in the vicinity of the USC main campus: [Jefferson/USC Station](/wiki/Jefferson/USC_(LACMTA_station) "Jefferson/USC (LACMTA station)"), [Expo Park/USC Station](/wiki/Expo_Park/USC_(LACMTA_station) "Expo Park/USC (LACMTA station)"), and [Vermont/Expo Station](/wiki/Vermont/Expo_(LACMTA_station) "Vermont/Expo (LACMTA station)").[[36]](#cite_note-36) The [Metro J Line](/wiki/J_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "J Line (Los Angeles Metro)") bus service serves both the University Park campus at [37th Street/USC station](/wiki/37th_Street/USC_station "37th Street/USC station") and the Health Sciences campus at [LA General Medical Center station](/wiki/LA_General_Medical_Center_station "LA General Medical Center station").[[37]](#cite_note-37) In addition, both campuses are served by several [Metro](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority") and municipal bus routes.

### Former agricultural college campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Former agricultural college campus")]

[Chaffey College](/wiki/Chaffey_College "Chaffey College") was founded in 1883 in the city of [Ontario, California](/wiki/Ontario,_California "Ontario, California"), as an agricultural college [branch campus](/wiki/Branch_campus "Branch campus") of USC under the name of Chaffey College of Agriculture of the University of Southern California. USC ran the Chaffey College of Agriculture until financial troubles closed the school in 1901. In 1906, the school was reopened by the municipal and regional government and thus officially separated from USC. Renamed as Chaffey College, it now exists as a [community college](/wiki/Community_college "Community college") as part of the [California Community College System](/wiki/California_Community_College_System "California Community College System").

## Organization and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Organization and administration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/USC-Bovard-1921.jpg/250px-USC-Bovard-1921.jpg)](/wiki/File:USC-Bovard-1921.jpg)

Bovard Hall, shortly after completion in 1921. The streets later became pedestrian-only.

USC is a private [public-benefit nonprofit corporation](/wiki/Public-benefit_nonprofit_corporation "Public-benefit nonprofit corporation") controlled by a [board of trustees](/wiki/Board_of_trustees "Board of trustees") composed of 50 voting members and several life trustees, honorary trustees, and trustees [emeriti](/wiki/Emeritus "Emeritus") who do not vote. Voting members of the board of trustees are elected for five-year terms. One-fifth of the Trustees stand for re-election each year, and votes are cast only by the trustees not standing for election. Trustees tend to be high-ranking executives of large corporations (both domestic and international), successful alumni, members of the upper echelons of university administration, or some combination of the three.

The university administration consists of a president, a [provost](/wiki/Provost_(education) "Provost (education)"), several vice-presidents of various departments, a treasurer, a [chief information officer](/wiki/Chief_Information_Officer "Chief Information Officer"), and an [athletic director](/wiki/Athletic_director "Athletic director").[[38]](#cite_note-Senior_Administration_of_the_University_of_Southern_California-38) The current president is [Beong-Soo Kim](/wiki/Beong-Soo_Kim "Beong-Soo Kim") who on July 1, 2025, succeeded [Carol Folt](/wiki/Carol_Folt "Carol Folt").[[39]](#cite_note-LAT_2018-12-12-39)[[40]](#cite_note-President_of_the_University_of_Southern_California-40)

The USC Dana and David Dornsife College of Letters, Arts, and Sciences, the Graduate School, and the twenty professional schools are each led by an academic [dean](/wiki/Academic_administration "Academic administration").[[41]](#cite_note-Academic_Deans_of_the_University_of_Southern_California-41) USC occasionally awards [emeritus](/wiki/Emeritus "Emeritus") titles to former administrators. There are six administrators emeriti. The University of Southern California's twenty professional schools include the [USC Leventhal School of Accounting](/wiki/USC_Leventhal_School_of_Accounting "USC Leventhal School of Accounting"), [USC School of Architecture](/wiki/USC_School_of_Architecture "USC School of Architecture"), USC Roski School of Art and Design, [USC Iovine and Young Academy](/wiki/USC_Jimmy_Iovine_and_Andre_Young_Academy "USC Jimmy Iovine and Andre Young Academy"), [USC Marshall School of Business](/wiki/USC_Marshall_School_of_Business "USC Marshall School of Business"), [USC School of Cinematic Arts](/wiki/USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts "USC School of Cinematic Arts"), [USC Annenberg School for Communication and Journalism](/wiki/USC_Annenberg_School_for_Communication_and_Journalism "USC Annenberg School for Communication and Journalism"), [USC Glorya Kaufman School of Dance](/wiki/USC_Kaufman_School_of_Dance "USC Kaufman School of Dance"), [Herman Ostrow School of Dentistry of USC](/wiki/Herman_Ostrow_School_of_Dentistry_of_USC "Herman Ostrow School of Dentistry of USC"), [USC School of Dramatic Arts](/wiki/USC_School_of_Dramatic_Arts "USC School of Dramatic Arts"), [USC Rossier School of Education](/wiki/USC_Rossier_School_of_Education "USC Rossier School of Education"), [USC Viterbi School of Engineering](/wiki/USC_Viterbi_School_of_Engineering "USC Viterbi School of Engineering"), [USC Leonard Davis School of Gerontology](/wiki/USC_Davis_School_of_Gerontology "USC Davis School of Gerontology"), [USC Gould School of Law](/wiki/USC_Gould_School_of_Law "USC Gould School of Law"), [Keck School of Medicine of USC](/wiki/Keck_School_of_Medicine_of_USC "Keck School of Medicine of USC"), [USC Thornton School of Music](/wiki/USC_Thornton_School_of_Music "USC Thornton School of Music"), [USC School of Pharmacy](/wiki/USC_School_of_Pharmacy "USC School of Pharmacy"), USC Bovard College, [USC Sol Price School of Public Policy](/wiki/USC_Price_School_of_Public_Policy "USC Price School of Public Policy"), and [USC Suzanne Dworak-Peck School of Social ### Student government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Student government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Gwynn_Wilson_Student_Union%2C_University_of_Southern_California.jpg/250px-Gwynn_Wilson_Student_Union%2C_University_of_Southern_California.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gwynn_Wilson_Student_Union,_University_of_Southern_California.jpg)

The Gwynn Wilson Student Union on the University Park campus

The Undergraduate Student Government (USG) is the official representative government of the undergraduate students at USC. It consists of a popularly elected president and vice president who lead an appointed executive cabinet, a popularly elected legislative branch, and judicial oversight. The executive cabinet oversees funding, communications, programming, and advocacy work. All USG activities are funded by the student activity fee. In addition to USG, residents within university housing are represented and governed by the Residential Housing Association (RHA), which is divided by residence hall. The Graduate Student Government (GSG) consists of senators elected by the students of each school proportional to its enrollment and its activities are funded by a graduate and professional student activity fee.

### Department of Public Safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Department of Public Safety")]

The USC Department of Public Safety (DPS) is one of the largest campus law enforcement agencies in the United States,[[73]](#cite_note-73) currently employing over 300 full-time personnel, including approximately 96 armed Public Safety Officers, 120 unarmed Community Service Officers, 60 CCTV monitors and dispatchers,[[74]](#cite_note-74) and 30 part-time student workers.[[75]](#cite_note-75) DPS's patrol and response jurisdiction includes a 2.5 square mile area around each USC campus.[[76]](#cite_note-76) The Department of Public Safety headquarters is on the University Park campus, and there are substations in the University Village and on the Health Sciences campus.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The department operates 24 hours a day, 365 days a year. All USC Public Safety Officers are required to be police academy graduates[[78]](#cite_note-78) so that under California Penal Code statute they can be granted peace officer power of arrest authority while on duty, enforce state laws and local city municipal codes, and investigate crimes.[[79]](#cite_note-79) DPS is overseen by an independent advisory board of 21 faculty, staff, student and community members appointed by the USC president. The board reviews DPS performance, stop, and misconduct data, and conducts periodic assessments of DPS policies, practices, and operational performance.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

The department has a formal working relationship with the [Los Angeles Police Department](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department") (LAPD),[[81]](#cite_note-81) which includes USC paying for newly hired Public Safety Officers to attend the six month-long Los Angeles Police Academy.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) A special joint USC/LAPD crime task force composed of USC DPS personnel and approximately 40 selected Los Angeles police officers, including a dedicated specially trained LAPD [SWAT](/wiki/SWAT "SWAT") team,[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) is assigned exclusively to the USC campus community to address crime and quality of life issues.

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Academics")]

Main article: [University of Southern California academics](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California_academics "University of Southern California academics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/USC_Law.jpg/250px-USC_Law.jpg)](/wiki/File:USC_Law.jpg)

The law school building is one example of [Brutalist architecture](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture") on USC's main campus.

USC is a large, primarily residential research university.[[86]](#cite_note-Carnegie-86) The majority of the student body was undergraduate until 2007, when graduate student enrollment began to exceed undergraduate.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The four-year, full-time undergraduate instructional program is classified as "balanced arts & sciences/professions" with a high graduate coexistence. Admissions are characterized as "most selective, lower transfer in"; 95 undergraduate majors and 147 academic and professional minors are offered.[[86]](#cite_note-Carnegie-86)[[88]](#cite_note-Catalogue-88) The graduate program is classified as "comprehensive" and offers 134 master's, doctoral, and professional degrees through twenty professional schools.[[86]](#cite_note-Carnegie-86)[[88]](#cite_note-Catalogue-88) USC is accredited by the [Western Association of Schools and Colleges - Senior College and University Commission](/wiki/Western_Association_of_Schools_and_Colleges_-_Senior_College_and_University_Commission "Western Association of Schools and Colleges - Senior College and University Commission").[[89]](#cite_note-89) USC's academic departments fall either under the general [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts "Liberal arts") and [sciences](/wiki/Science "Science") of the Dana and David Dornsife College of Letters, Arts, and Sciences for undergraduate programs, the Graduate School for graduate programs, or the university's 20 professional schools.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Bovard_Auditorium_at_dusk%2C_University_of_Southern_California.jpg/250px-Bovard_Auditorium_at_dusk%2C_University_of_Southern_California.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bovard_Auditorium_at_dusk,_University_of_Southern_California.jpg)

Bovard Auditorium

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Vivian_Hall%2C_USC.jpg/250px-Vivian_Hall%2C_USC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vivian_Hall,_USC.jpg)

Vivian Hall, part of the [Viterbi School of Engineering](/wiki/USC_Viterbi_School_of_Engineering "USC Viterbi School of Engineering") complex

The USC Dana and David Dornsife College of Letters, Arts, and Sciences, the oldest and largest of the USC schools, grants undergraduate degrees in more than 180 majors and minors across the humanities, social sciences, and natural/physical sciences, and offers doctoral and masters programs in more than twenty fields.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Dornsife College is responsible for the general education program for all USC undergraduates and houses a full-time faculty of almost 1,000, nearly 8,000 undergraduate majors (over a third of the total USC undergraduate population), and 1,300 doctoral students. In addition to thirty academic departments, the college also houses dozens of research centers and institutes. In the 2008–2009 academic year, 4,400 undergraduate degrees and 5,500 advanced degrees were awarded. Formerly called "USC College of Letters, Arts & Sciences", the college received a $200 million gift from USC trustees Dana and David Dornsife on March 23, 2011, after which the college was renamed in their honor, following the naming pattern of other professional schools and departments at the university.[[92]](#cite_note-92) All PhD degrees awarded at USC and most master's degrees are under the jurisdiction of the Graduate School.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Professional degrees are awarded by each of the respective professional schools.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts_03.jpg/250px-USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts_03.jpg)

The [USC School of Cinematic Arts](/wiki/USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts "USC School of Cinematic Arts")

[The School of Cinematic Arts](/wiki/USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts "USC School of Cinematic Arts"), the oldest and largest film school in the country, confers degrees in six different programs.[[94]](#cite_note-LATcntv-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) As the university administration considered cinematic skills too valuable to be kept to film industry professionals, the school opened its classes to the university at large in 1998.[[96]](#cite_note-96) In 2001, the film school added an [Interactive Media & Games Division](/wiki/USC_Interactive_Media_%26_Games_Division "USC Interactive Media & Games Division") studying stereoscopic cinema, panoramic cinema, immersive cinema, interactive cinema, video games, virtual reality, and [mobile](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") media. In September 2006, George Lucas donated $175 million to expand the film school, which at the time was the largest single donation to USC (and its fifth over $100 million). The donation will be used to build new structures and expand the faculty.[[97]](#cite_note-97) The acceptance rate to the School of Cinematic Arts has consistently remained between 4–6% for the past several years.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Watt_Hall%2C_USC.jpg/250px-Watt_Hall%2C_USC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Watt_Hall,_USC.jpg)

Watt Hall houses the [USC School of Architecture](/wiki/USC_School_of_Architecture "USC School of Architecture") and the Roski School of Art and Design.

The [USC School of Architecture](/wiki/USC_School_of_Architecture "USC School of Architecture") was established in 1916, the first in [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"). From at least 1972 to 1976, and likely for a number of years prior to 1972, it was called The School of Architecture and Fine Art. The School of Fine Art (known as SOFA for a number of years after Architecture and Fine Art separated) was eventually named the Roski School of Fine Arts in 2006 during a ceremony to open the then-new Masters of Fine Art building, which occupies the previous and completely refurbished Lucky Blue Jean factory. This small department grew rapidly with the help of the Allied Architects of Los Angeles. A separate School of Architecture was organized in September 1925. The school has been home to teachers such as [Richard Neutra](/wiki/Richard_Neutra "Richard Neutra"), Ralph Knowles, James Steele, [A. Quincy Jones](/wiki/A._Quincy_Jones "A. Quincy Jones"), [William Pereira](/wiki/William_Pereira "William Pereira") and [Pierre Koenig](/wiki/Pierre_Koenig "Pierre Koenig"). The school of architecture also claims notable alumni [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry"), [Jon Jerde](/wiki/Jon_Jerde "Jon Jerde"), [Thom Mayne](/wiki/Thom_Mayne "Thom Mayne"), [Raphael Soriano](/wiki/Raphael_Soriano "Raphael Soriano"), [Gregory Ain](/wiki/Gregory_Ain "Gregory Ain"), and Pierre Koenig. Two of the alumni have become [Pritzker Prize](/wiki/Pritzker_Prize "Pritzker Prize") winners. In 2006, [Qingyun Ma](/wiki/Qingyun_Ma "Qingyun Ma"), a distinguished Shanghai-based architect, was named dean of the school.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

The [Andrew and Erna Viterbi School of Engineering](/wiki/Viterbi_School_of_Engineering "Viterbi School of Engineering") is headed by Dean [Yannis Yortsos](/wiki/Yanis_C._Yortsos "Yanis C. Yortsos"). Previously known as the USC School of Engineering, it was renamed on March 2, 2004, in honor of [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm") co-founder [Andrew Viterbi](/wiki/Andrew_Viterbi "Andrew Viterbi") and his wife Erna, who had donated $52 million to the school. Viterbi School of Engineering has been ranked No. 11 and No. 9 in the United States in *U.S. News & World Report*'s engineering rankings for 2018 and 2019 respectively.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/052707-020-USCAnnenberg.jpg/250px-052707-020-USCAnnenberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:052707-020-USCAnnenberg.jpg)

The Annenberg School for Communication & Journalism

The [Annenberg School for Communication & Journalism](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California_Annenberg_School_for_Communication "University of Southern California Annenberg School for Communication"), founded in 1971, is one of the two communication programs in the country endowed by [Walter Annenberg](/wiki/Walter_Annenberg "Walter Annenberg") (the other is at the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania")). The School of Journalism, which became part of the School for Communication in 1994,[[100]](#cite_note-100) features a core curriculum that requires students to devote themselves equally to print, broadcast and online media for the first year of study. The journalism school consistently ranks among the nation's top undergraduate journalism schools.[[101]](#cite_note-101) USC's Annenberg School's endowment rose from $7.5 million to $218 million between 1996 and 2007.[[102]](#cite_note-102) In 2015, the new building named for [Wallis Annenberg](/wiki/Wallis_Annenberg "Wallis Annenberg") started serving all faculty and students.

The [Herman Ostrow School of Dentistry](/wiki/Herman_Ostrow_School_of_Dentistry_of_USC "Herman Ostrow School of Dentistry of USC") at the University of Southern California was established in 1897 as The College of Dentistry, and today, awards undergraduate and graduate degrees. Headed by Dean Avishai Sadan, the school traditionally has maintained five Divisions: Academic Affairs & Student Life, Clinical Affairs, Continuing Education, Research, and Community Health Programs and Hospital Affairs. In 2006, the [USC Department of Physical Therapy and Biokinesiology](/wiki/USC_Division_of_Biokinesiology_and_Physical_Therapy "USC Division of Biokinesiology and Physical Therapy"), and the USC Department of Occupational Science and Occupational Therapy, which both had previously been organized as "Independent Health Professions" programs at the USC College of Letters, Arts, and Sciences, were administratively aligned under the School of Dentistry and renamed "Divisions," bringing the total number of Divisions at the School of Dentistry to seven. In 2010, alumnus Herman Ostrow donated $35 million to name the school the Herman Ostrow School of Dentistry. In 2013, the school introduced an eighth division, and in 2014, a $20 million gift endowed and named the USC Mrs. T.H. Chan Division of Occupational Science and Occupational Therapy.

USC collaborated with [Shanghai Jiao Tong University](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiao_Tong_University "Shanghai Jiao Tong University") to offer the USC Executive MBA program in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"). USC Dornsife also operates two international study centers in Paris and Madrid. The [Marshall School of Business](/wiki/Marshall_School_of_Business "Marshall School of Business") has satellite campuses in [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California") and [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"). In 2012, USC established the [Glorya Kaufman School of Dance](/wiki/USC_Kaufman_School_of_Dance "USC Kaufman School of Dance"), the university's first new school in forty years,[[103]](#cite_note-School_of_Dance-103) which was a gift from philanthropist [Glorya Kaufman](/wiki/Glorya_Kaufman "Glorya Kaufman").[[103]](#cite_note-School_of_Dance-103) The USC Kaufman School offers individual classes in technique, performance, choreography, production, theory and history open to all students at USC.[[104]](#cite_note-USC_Dance_website-104) In the fall of 2015, the USC Glorya Kaufman School of Dance began to offer a Bachelor of Fine Arts degree to a select number of undergraduates who wish to pursue dance as their major.[[104]](#cite_note-USC_Dance_website-104) This four-year professional degree is housed in the state-of-the-art [Glorya Kaufman International Dance Center](/wiki/Glorya_Kaufman_International_Dance_Center "Glorya Kaufman International Dance Center").[[104]](#cite_note-USC_Dance_website-104) In 2015, USC established the Bovard College, which offers graduate-level programs in Human Resource Management, Project Management, and Criminal Justice. The college is named after Emma Bovard, who was one of the first students to enroll at USC in 1880.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

### University library system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: University library system")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c3/USCLeavey2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:USCLeavey2007.jpg)

The Leavey Library, completed in the mid-1990s, reflected a shift to earlier Romanesque architecture in that era. It is USC's newest library.

Main article: [University of Southern California libraries](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California_libraries "University of Southern California libraries")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Doheny_Library_interior.jpg/250px-Doheny_Library_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doheny_Library_interior.jpg)

Interior of the [Doheny Memorial Library](/wiki/Doheny_Library "Doheny Library").

The USC Libraries are among the oldest private academic research libraries in [California](/wiki/California "California"). For more than a century USC has been building collections in support of the university's teaching and research interests. Especially noteworthy collections include [American literature](/wiki/American_literature "American literature"), Cinema-Television including the [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") studio archives, European [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy"), [gerontology](/wiki/Gerontology "Gerontology"), German exile literature, [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations"), [Korean studies](/wiki/Korean_studies "Korean studies"), studies of Latin America, [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history"), [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California") history, and the University Archives.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

The USC Warner Bros. Archives is the largest single studio collection in the world. Donated in 1977 to the University of Southern California's School of Cinematic Arts by Warner Communications, the WBA houses departmental records that detail Warner Bros. activities from the studio's first major feature, *My Four Years in Germany* (1918), to its sale to Seven Arts in 1968. Announced in June 2006, the testimony of 52,000 survivors, rescuers, and others involved in [The Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") is housed in the USC College of Letters, Arts & Sciences as a part of the [USC Shoah Foundation Institute for Visual History and Education](/wiki/USC_Shoah_Foundation_Institute_for_Visual_History_and_Education "USC Shoah Foundation Institute for Visual History and Education").[[107]](#cite_note-107) The Shoah Foundation is on the 4th floor of Leavy Library.

In addition to the Shoah Foundation, the USC Libraries digital collection highlights include photographs from the California Historical Society, Korean American Archives Automobile Club of Southern California, and the Chinese Historical Society of Southern California. The USC Digital Library[[108]](#cite_note-108) provides a wealth of primary and original source material in a variety of formats. In October 2010, the collections at [ONE National Gay & Lesbian Archives](/wiki/ONE_National_Gay_%26_Lesbian_Archives "ONE National Gay & Lesbian Archives"), the largest repository for documents from the [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") community in the world, became a part of the USC Libraries system.[[109]](#cite_note-109) The collections at ONE include over two million archival items documenting LGBT history including periodicals; books; film, video and audio recordings; photographs; artworks; ephemera, such clothing, costumes, and buttons; organizational records; and personal paper.

USC's 22 libraries and other archives hold nearly 4 million printed volumes, 6 million items in [microform](/wiki/Microform "Microform"), and 3 million [photographs](/wiki/Photographs "Photographs") and subscribe to more than 30,000 current serial titles, nearly 44,000 feet (13,000 m) of manuscripts and archives, and subscribe to over 120 electronic databases and more than 14,000 journals in print and electronic formats. Annually, reference transactions number close to 50,000 and approximately 1,100 instructional presentations are made to 16,000 participants.[[110]](#cite_note-110) The University of Southern California Library system is among the top 35 largest university library systems in the United States.[[111]](#cite_note-111) The Leavey Library is the undergraduate library and is open 24 hours a day. The newly open basement has many discussion tables for students to share thoughts and have group discussions. The Edward L. Doheny, Jr. Memorial Library is the main research library on campus.

USC has a total enrollment of roughly 47,500 students, of which 20,000 are at the undergraduate and 27,500 at the graduate and professional levels.[[3]](#cite_note-About_USC-3) Approximately 53% of students are female and 47% are male. For the entering first-year class in 2020, 43% of incoming students are drawn from California, 42% from the rest of the United States, and 15% from abroad.[[125]](#cite_note-127) In the academic year 2014–2015, USC's student body encompasses 12,300 international students, the second most out of all universities in the United States.[[126]](#cite_note-128) Of the regularly enrolled international students, the most represented countries/regions are China (Hong Kong, Macau, and Taiwan not included), India, South Korea, and Taiwan, in that order.

Like other private universities, the nominal cost of attendance is high; however, the university's large endowment and significant revenue streams allow it to offer generous financial aid packages. As of 2025, the average aid package was $71,169 among those who received aid.[[127]](#cite_note-129) USC also offer some very competitive and highly valued merit-based scholarships (the full-tuition, four-year Mork Family, Stamps and Trustee scholarships; the half-tuition Presidential Scholarship; the one-quarter tuition Deans Scholarship),[[128]](#cite_note-130) with only 5.5% of scholarship applicants being selected as finalists for the final interview invitation at the USC campus in spring.[[129]](#cite_note-131) This makes USC one of the highest-ranked universities to offer half-tuition and full-tuition merit-based scholarships.[[130]](#cite_note-132) These factors have propelled USC into being the 4th most economically diverse university in the nation.[[131]](#cite_note-133)

USC enrolls one of the highest number of National Merit Scholars of any university, offering finalists in the program its half-tuition Presidential Scholarship.[[132]](#cite_note-134) As of 2021, about 72% of the student body receives about $810 million in financial aid annually.[[133]](#cite_note-135) Twenty percent of admitted and attending students are SCions, or students with familial ties to USC, while 14 percent are the first generation in their family to attend any form of college. Twenty-four percent of undergraduates at USC are [Pell Grant](/wiki/Pell_Grant "Pell Grant")-eligible, which is defined by having come from a family household income of less than $50,000.[[134]](#cite_note-136) There are over 375,000 living [Trojan alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Southern_California_people "List of University of Southern California people").[[3]](#cite_note-About_USC-3) The USC-MSA reference is a numbering system developed by the [Muslim Students' Association](/wiki/Muslim_Students%27_Association "Muslim Students' Association") of the University of Southern California to access their database of the [six major Hadith collections](/wiki/Six_major_Hadith_collections "Six major Hadith collections"). Although the project currently parked, the referencing remains widely used throughout the Internet.[[135]](#cite_note-137)

USC is ranked by *U.S. News & World Report* as "Most Selective,"[[142]](#cite_note-:0-144) and *Princeton Review* rates its admissions selectivity of 98 out of 99.[[143]](#cite_note-145) Over 70,000 students applied for admission to the undergraduate class entering in 2021, with 12% being admitted.[[144]](#cite_note-146)

Among enrolled freshman for Fall 2019, the [interquartile](/wiki/Interquartile_range "Interquartile range") (middle 50%) range of [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") scores was 670–740 for evidence-based reading and writing, 680–790 for math, and 1370–1520 for the composite.[[138]](#cite_note-CommonDataSet2019-20-140) The middle 50% [ACT](/wiki/ACT_(test) "ACT (test)") score range was 28–34 for math, 32–35 for English, and 31–34 for the composite.[[138]](#cite_note-CommonDataSet2019-20-140) USC was ranked the 10th most applied to university in the nation for fall 2014 by *U.S. News & World Report*.[[145]](#cite_note-147) Admission is [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") for domestic applicants.[[146]](#cite_note-148)

## Faculty and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Faculty and research")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/052707-022-NorrisCinema-USC.jpg/250px-052707-022-NorrisCinema-USC.jpg)](/wiki/File:052707-022-NorrisCinema-USC.jpg)

The Eileen L. Norris Cinema Theatre, where the [THX](/wiki/THX "THX") sound system was first developed and installed by [Tomlinson Holman](/wiki/Tomlinson_Holman "Tomlinson Holman").[[147]](#cite_note-USCselftour-149)

The university is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity".[[86]](#cite_note-Carnegie-86) According to the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), USC spent $891 million on research and development in 2018, ranking it 23rd in the nation.[[148]](#cite_note-150)[[3]](#cite_note-About_USC-3) USC employs approximately 4,706 full-time faculty, 1,816 part-time faculty, 16,614 staff members, and 4,817 student workers.[[149]](#cite_note-151) 350 postdoctoral fellows are supported along with over 800 medical residents.[[150]](#cite_note-152) Among the USC faculty, 17 are members of the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences"), 16 are members of the [National Academy of Medicine](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Medicine "National Academy of Medicine"), 37 are members of the [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering"), 97 are members of the [American Association for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/American_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "American Association for the Advancement of Science"), and 34 are members of the [American Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "American Academy of Arts and Sciences"),[[151]](#cite_note-153)[[152]](#cite_note-154) 5 to the [American Philosophical Society](/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society"),[[153]](#cite_note-MiscAcadMembers-155) and 14 to the [National Academy of Public Administration](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Public_Administration_(United_States) "National Academy of Public Administration (United States)").[[153]](#cite_note-MiscAcadMembers-155) 29 USC faculty are listed as among the "Highly Cited" in the [Institute for Scientific Information](/wiki/Institute_for_Scientific_Information "Institute for Scientific Information") database.[[154]](#cite_note-HighlyCitedUSC-156) [George Olah](/wiki/George_Olah "George Olah") won the 1994 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry")[[155]](#cite_note-157) and was the founding director of the [Loker Hydrocarbon Research Institute](/wiki/Loker_Hydrocarbon_Research_Institute "Loker Hydrocarbon Research Institute"). [Leonard Adleman](/wiki/Leonard_Adleman "Leonard Adleman") won the [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") in 2003.[[156]](#cite_note-158) [Arieh Warshel](/wiki/Arieh_Warshel "Arieh Warshel") won the 2013 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry").[[157]](#cite_note-159)

The university also supports the [Pacific Council on International Policy](/wiki/Pacific_Council_on_International_Policy "Pacific Council on International Policy") through joint programming, leadership collaboration, and facilitated connections among students, faculty, and Pacific Council members.[[158]](#cite_note-160) The university has two [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation")–funded Engineering Research Centers: the [Integrated Media Systems Center](/wiki/Integrated_Media_Systems_Center "Integrated Media Systems Center") and the [Center for Biomimetic Microelectronic Systems](/wiki/Center_for_Biomimetic_Microelectronic_Systems "Center for Biomimetic Microelectronic Systems").[[159]](#cite_note-161) The [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security") selected USC as its first Homeland Security Center of Excellence. Since 1991, USC has been the headquarters of the [NSF](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") and [USGS](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") funded [Southern California Earthquake Center](/wiki/Southern_California_Earthquake_Center "Southern California Earthquake Center") (SCEC). The University of Southern California is a founding and charter member of [CENIC](/wiki/CENIC "CENIC"), the Corporation for Education Network Initiatives in California, the nonprofit organization, which provides extremely high-performance Internet-based networking to California's K-20 research and education community. USC researcher [Jonathan Postel](/wiki/Jonathan_Postel "Jonathan Postel") was an editor of communications-protocol for the fledgling internet, also known as [ARPANET](/wiki/ARPANET "ARPANET"), for which USC was one of the earliest nodes.[[160]](#cite_note-:1-162)

In July 2016, USC became home to the world's most powerful [quantum computer](/wiki/Quantum_computer "Quantum computer"), housed in a super-cooled, magnetically shielded facility at the USC [Information Sciences Institute](/wiki/Information_Sciences_Institute "Information Sciences Institute"),[[161]](#cite_note-163)[[162]](#cite_note-164) the only other commercially available quantum computing system operated jointly by [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") and [Google](/wiki/Google "Google").
