In February 2023, USC Graduate Student Workers voted 93% to unionize with the [United Auto Workers](/wiki/United_Auto_Workers "United Auto Workers"), becoming the first academic worker union at a private university in Los Angeles.[[163]](#cite_note-165) Workers voted to form a union by a vote of 1,599–122.[[164]](#cite_note-166) The union campaign of over 3,000 research assistants, teaching assistants, and assistant lecturers garnered support from numerous California elected officials, including United States senator [Alex Padilla](/wiki/Alex_Padilla "Alex Padilla")[[165]](#cite_note-167) and Los Angeles City Councilman [Hugo Soto-Martinez](/wiki/Hugo_Soto-Martinez "Hugo Soto-Martinez").[[166]](#cite_note-168)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [USC Trojans](/wiki/USC_Trojans "USC Trojans")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/11-11-06-GalenCenter-inside.jpg/250px-11-11-06-GalenCenter-inside.jpg)](/wiki/File:11-11-06-GalenCenter-inside.jpg)

The [Galen Center](/wiki/Galen_Center "Galen Center"), home of USC basketball and volleyball

The USC Trojans participate in the [NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") (FBS) as part of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference").[[167]](#cite_note-169) USC student athletes have won 123 total team national championships, 97 for men and 26 for women, including non-NCAA championships. Of this total, 80 and 14 are NCAA national championships for men and women, respectively. The NCAA does not include [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football") championships in its calculation. Although there are multiple organizations that name national champions, USC claims 11 football championships. The men's 361 individual championships are the second-best in the nation and 53 ahead of third place, the [Texas Longhorns](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns "Texas Longhorns"). USC's cross-town rival is the [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles") (UCLA), with whom there is fierce athletic and scholastic competition. USC's rivalry with [Notre Dame](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame "University of Notre Dame") predates the [UCLA rivalry](/wiki/UCLA%E2%80%93USC_rivalry "UCLA–USC rivalry") by three years. The Notre Dame rivalry stems mainly from the annual [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") game played between these two universities and is considered one of the greatest rivalries in college athletics.[[168]](#cite_note-170)

USC has won 113 NCAA team championships, third most behind Stanford (135)[[169]](#cite_note-171) and cross-town rival UCLA (123).[[17]](#cite_note-ncaa-championship-17) The Trojans have also won at least one national team title in 26 consecutive years (1959–1960 to 1984–1985). USC won the National College All-Sports Championship, an annual ranking by *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* of the country's top athletic programs, 6 times since its inception in 1971. Four Trojans have won the [James E. Sullivan Award](/wiki/James_E._Sullivan_Award "James E. Sullivan Award") as the top amateur athlete in America: diver [Sammy Lee](/wiki/Sammy_Lee_(diver) "Sammy Lee (diver)") (1953), shot putter [Parry O'Brien](/wiki/Parry_O%27Brien "Parry O'Brien") (1959), swimmer [John Naber](/wiki/John_Naber "John Naber") (1977) and swimmer [Janet Evans](/wiki/Janet_Evans "Janet Evans") (1989). From the 1904 [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") through the 2014 [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics"), 632 Trojan athletes have competed in the Games, taking home 144 [gold medals](/wiki/Gold_medals "Gold medals"), 93 [silver](/wiki/Silver_medal "Silver medal") and 72 [bronze](/wiki/Bronze_medal "Bronze medal").[[88]](#cite_note-Catalogue-88) If it were an independent country, USC would be ranked [13th in the world](/wiki/All-time_Olympic_Games_medal_table "All-time Olympic Games medal table") in 2016 in terms of medals.[[170]](#cite_note-USC_Olympics-172) Since 1912, USC is the only university in the world to have a gold medal-winning athlete in every summer Olympiad.[[88]](#cite_note-Catalogue-88)

### Men's sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Men's sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/USC_vs_University_of_Oregon_November_2019.png/250px-USC_vs_University_of_Oregon_November_2019.png)](/wiki/File:USC_vs_University_of_Oregon_November_2019.png)

The [Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum") during a USC football game.

In men's sports, USC has won 97 team national championships (84 NCAA titles) – more than any other school – and male athletes have won a record 303 individual NCAA titles. The Trojans have won 26 championships in [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field"), 21 in [tennis](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Tennis_Championship "NCAA Men's Tennis Championship"), 12 in [baseball](/wiki/College_World_Series "College World Series"), 9 in [swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") and [diving](/wiki/Diving_(sport) "Diving (sport)"), 9 in [water polo](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Water_Polo_Championship "NCAA Men's Water Polo Championship"), 6 in [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), 2 in indoor track and field, and 1 in [gymnastics](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Gymnastics_Championships "NCAA Men's Gymnastics Championships").[[c]](#endnote_Cnone) USC's men's basketball has appeared in the [NCAA tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Division I Men's Basketball Championship") 15 times, and made 2 NCAA [Final Four](/wiki/Final_Four "Final Four") appearances.

The [USC football program](/wiki/USC_Trojans_football "USC Trojans football") has historically ranked among the best in Division I FBS. The Trojans football team has won 11 national championships.[[171]](#cite_note-173) Eight players have won the [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy"). As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit), 537 Trojans have been taken in the [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft"), making it the school with the most NFL draft picks.[[19]](#cite_note-nfldraft-19)

### Women's sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Women's sports")]

Women's teams have earned 27 national championships. The Women of Troy have brought home 64 individual NCAA crowns. Two women athletes have won the [Honda-Broderick Cup](/wiki/Honda-Broderick_Cup "Honda-Broderick Cup") as the top collegiate woman athlete of the year: [Cheryl Miller](/wiki/Cheryl_Miller "Cheryl Miller") (1983–84) and [Angela Williams](/wiki/Angela_Williams_(sprinter_born_1980) "Angela Williams (sprinter born 1980)") (2001–02). Trojan women have won 8 [Honda Awards](/wiki/Honda_Sports_Award "Honda Sports Award"), as the top female athlete in their sport. The Women of Troy have won 7 championships in [tennis](/wiki/NCAA_Women%27s_Tennis_Championship "NCAA Women's Tennis Championship"), 6 in [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), 4 in [water polo](/wiki/NCAA_Women%27s_Water_Polo_Championship "NCAA Women's Water Polo Championship"), 3 in [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), 2 in [basketball](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Women%27s_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Division I Women's Basketball Championship"), 2 in [beach volleyball](/wiki/NCAA_Beach_Volleyball_Championship "NCAA Beach Volleyball Championship"), 1 in [swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") and [diving](/wiki/Diving_(sport) "Diving (sport)"), 2 in [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") and 2 in [soccer](/wiki/Soccer "Soccer").

## Traditions and student activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Traditions and student activities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Usctravelerandband.jpg/250px-Usctravelerandband.jpg)](/wiki/File:Usctravelerandband.jpg)

USC mascot Traveler with Trojan Warrior and the Spirit of Troy

As one of the oldest universities in California, the University of Southern California has a number of traditions. USC's official [fight song](/wiki/Fight_song "Fight song") is "[Fight On](/wiki/Fight_On "Fight On")", which was composed in 1922 by USC dental student Milo Sweet with lyrics by Sweet and Glen Grant.[[172]](#cite_note-174)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Main articles: [Notre Dame – USC rivalry](/wiki/Notre_Dame_%E2%80%93_USC_rivalry "Notre Dame – USC rivalry") and [UCLA–USC rivalry](/wiki/UCLA%E2%80%93USC_rivalry "UCLA–USC rivalry")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/120107-LA-USC-UCLA02.jpg/250px-120107-LA-USC-UCLA02.jpg)](/wiki/File:120107-LA-USC-UCLA02.jpg)

During the week prior to the traditional USC-UCLA rivalry football game, the [Tommy Trojan](/wiki/Tommy_Trojan "Tommy Trojan") statue is covered to prevent UCLA vandalism.

USC has rivalries with multiple schools. Although generally limited to football, USC has a major rivalry with [Notre Dame](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame "University of Notre Dame").[[173]](#cite_note-175) The annual game is played for the [Jeweled Shillelagh](/wiki/Jeweled_Shillelagh "Jeweled Shillelagh"). The rivalry has featured more national championship teams, Heisman trophy winners, All-Americans, and future NFL hall-of-famers than any other collegiate match-up. The two schools have kept the annual game on their schedules since 1926 (except 1942–44 because of World War II travel restrictions and 2020 because of the COVID-19 Pandemic) and the game is often referred to as the greatest intersectional rivalry in college football.[[174]](#cite_note-SI101305-176)[[175]](#cite_note-ESPN112406-177)[[176]](#cite_note-SI101205-178)[[177]](#cite_note-LAT102007-179)[[178]](#cite_note-PAC112006-180)

USC's most famous rival is UCLA with whom there is fierce athletic and scholastic competition. Both universities are in Los Angeles and approximately 10 miles (16 km) apart. Both schools were members of the [Pac-12 Conference](/wiki/Pac-12_Conference "Pac-12 Conference") for nearly a century until leaving for the Big Ten in 2024. Until 1982, the two schools also shared the same football stadium, the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum. The victor of the annual football game takes home the [Victory Bell](/wiki/Victory_Bell_(UCLA%E2%80%93USC) "Victory Bell (UCLA–USC)"). The Trojans and Bruins also compete in a year-long all-sports competition for the [Lexus Gauntlet Trophy](/wiki/Lexus_Gauntlet_Trophy "Lexus Gauntlet Trophy"). Pranks between [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA") and USC were commonplace several decades ago. Both universities have cracked down on pranks since a 1989 incident when USC students released hundreds of [crickets](/wiki/Cricket_(insect) "Cricket (insect)") into the [main UCLA library](/wiki/Powell_Library "Powell Library") during finals week.[[179]](#cite_note-181) Days before a clash between rivals [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA") and USC in 2009, the Bruins mascot was vandalized. It was splashed in cardinal and gold paint, USC's official colors, sparking memories of pranks played in the years earlier.[[180]](#cite_note-182) The week preceding the annual football matchup with UCLA is known as "Troy Week" and features a number of traditions including "The Ultimate Trojan Experience", Save Tommy Night, the CONQUEST! Bonfire, and all-night vigils by the [USC Helenes](/wiki/USC_Helenes "USC Helenes") and [Trojan Knights](/wiki/Trojan_Knights "Trojan Knights") to protect the campus from [UCLA Bruins](/wiki/UCLA_Bruins "UCLA Bruins").

In addition, USC has rivalries with other former Pac-12 schools, particularly the [Stanford Cardinal](/wiki/Stanford_Cardinal "Stanford Cardinal") as they were the only two private universities in the Pac-12 Conference and are situated at opposing regions of California, as well as being the two oldest private research universities in California, 1880 and 1891, respectively. Recently, a rivalry has begun to exist between USC and the University of Oregon because of the two universities' dominant football programs with each school often serving as the toughest match-up on the opponent's schedule.

### Mascots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Mascots")]

Traveler, a white [Andalusian horse](/wiki/Andalusian_horse "Andalusian horse"), is the university's official mascot. Traveler I first appeared at a football game in 1961 ridden by Richard Saukko. The current horse is known as Traveler IX.[[181]](#cite_note-183) [Tommy Trojan](/wiki/Tommy_Trojan "Tommy Trojan"), officially known as the Trojan Shrine, is a bronze statue in the model of a Trojan warrior at the center of campus. It is commonly mistaken as the school's official mascot. The statue was modeled after Trojan football players and is engraved with the ideal characteristics of a Trojan. It is a popular meeting point for students and a landmark for visitors.[[182]](#cite_note-184) In the 1940s, [George Tirebiter](/wiki/George_Tirebiter "George Tirebiter"), a car-chasing dog, was the most popular unofficial mascot. It gained fame among students after it bit the mascot of the UCLA Bruins. The dog was kept by the Trojan Knights and was known to chase down cars on Trousdale Parkway, which runs through campus. After the original dog died, three others succeeded it. A statue was built in 2006 to honor the unofficial mascot.[[183]](#cite_note-185)

### Marching band

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Marching band")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/2008-0830-USC-UVA-TMB-drummajor.jpg/250px-2008-0830-USC-UVA-TMB-drummajor.jpg)](/wiki/File:2008-0830-USC-UVA-TMB-drummajor.jpg)

The drum major of the Spirit of Troy wears a more elaborate uniform and conducts the band with a sword.

[The Spirit of Troy](/wiki/Spirit_of_Troy "Spirit of Troy") is USC's [marching band](/wiki/Marching_band "Marching band") and has been featured in at least ten major [movies](/wiki/Film "Film") and performed in both the [1932](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics") and [1984 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") in Los Angeles.[[184]](#cite_note-186) They have also performed on television shows and with other musicians. The band performed on the [title track](/wiki/Tusk_(song) "Tusk (song)") of the 1979 [Fleetwood Mac](/wiki/Fleetwood_Mac "Fleetwood Mac") album *[Tusk](/wiki/Tusk_(album) "Tusk (album)")*, which went on to be a [multi-platinum record](/wiki/Platinum_record "Platinum record"). The band performed during halftime at [Super Bowl XXI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXI "Super Bowl XXI") in 1987 and [Super Bowl XXII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXII "Super Bowl XXII") in 1988. In 1990, the band performed live on *[America's Funniest Home Videos](/wiki/America%27s_Funniest_Home_Videos "America's Funniest Home Videos")*. Additionally, the band later played on another multi-platinum Fleetwood Mac album, *[The Dance](/wiki/The_Dance_(Fleetwood_Mac_album) "The Dance (Fleetwood Mac album)")* (1997).[[185]](#cite_note-FleetwoodPlat-187) The Spirit of Troy is the only collegiate band to have two platinum records.[[186]](#cite_note-DT081804-188)[[187]](#cite_note-TMBnotes-189)

In recent years, the band has appeared at the [2009 Grammy Awards](/wiki/51st_Grammy_Award "51st Grammy Award"), accompanying [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead"); on the [2009 Academy Awards](/wiki/81st_Academy_Awards "81st Academy Awards") with [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") and [Hugh Jackman](/wiki/Hugh_Jackman "Hugh Jackman"); and during the finale of *[American Idol](/wiki/American_Idol "American Idol")* 2008, backing [Renaldo Lapuz](/wiki/Renaldo_Lapuz "Renaldo Lapuz") in instrumentation of his original song, "We're Brothers Forever".[[188]](#cite_note-LAT020709-190)[[189]](#cite_note-LAT022609-191)[[190]](#cite_note-LAT052108-192) In 2009, the band played on the show *[Dancing with the Stars](/wiki/Dancing_with_the_Stars "Dancing with the Stars")*.[[191]](#cite_note-MSNBC092909-193) The USC band was only one of two American groups invited to march in the Hong Kong [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") parade in 2003 and 2004. The Trojan Marching Band performed at the 2005 World Expo in [Nagoya, Japan](/wiki/Nagoya,_Aichi "Nagoya, Aichi"). In May 2006, the Trojan Marching Band traveled to Italy, performing once in Florence, and twice in Rome (including in front of the Coliseum). The band has also, for many years, performed the [1812 Overture](/wiki/1812_Overture "1812 Overture") with the [Los Angeles Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Los Angeles Philharmonic Orchestra") (or occasionally with other orchestras) each year at the [Hollywood Bowl](/wiki/Hollywood_Bowl "Hollywood Bowl") "Tchaikovsky Spectacular."[[192]](#cite_note-LAT082707-194)

### Spirit groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Spirit groups")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/091507-USCNeb-SongGirls.jpg/250px-091507-USCNeb-SongGirls.jpg)](/wiki/File:091507-USCNeb-SongGirls.jpg)

The Song Girls celebrating a [USC Trojans](/wiki/USC_Trojans "USC Trojans") football victory

#### Song Girls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Song Girls")]

Founded in 1967, the USC Song Girls appear at football, basketball, and volleyball games as well as other sporting events, rallies, and university and alumni functions.[[193]](#cite_note-LAT_2019-1-18-195)[[194]](#cite_note-196) The squad also performs internationally. The squad has traveled to [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [China](/wiki/China "China") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), most recently having traveled to [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), Italy to perform at the [2015 World Expo](/wiki/2015_World_Expo "2015 World Expo") on America's Independence Day. Unlike other college cheer teams, Song Girls are primarily a dance squad and do not perform gymnastics, stunts, or lead cheers.[[193]](#cite_note-LAT_2019-1-18-195)[[195]](#cite_note-197) The Song Girls perform to the music of and often appear with The Spirit of Troy. Together with the Trojan Marching Band, they are a visible public face of the university and function as the ambassadors of spirit and goodwill for the Trojan Family.

#### Yell Leaders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Yell Leaders")]

Lindley Bothwell founded the USC Yell Leading Squad in 1919 in his first year as a student at USC. He felt that together, with a few friends, he could aid in "firing up" the crowd during football games.[[196]](#cite_note-ABOUT_«_USC_Spirit_Leaders-198) The USC Yell Leaders worked closely with The Spirit of Troy and the Song Girls to lead cheers and perform stunts to rally Trojan fans at football, basketball, and volleyball games. The sweater-clad team consisted of all men for most of its existence, though the squad later opened itself up to applicants from both sexes and did feature one female Yell Leader in 1998.[[197]](#cite_note-199) They were disbanded by the university after the 2005–06 season and replaced by the co-ed Spirit Leaders.[[198]](#cite_note-200)

#### Spirit Leaders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Spirit Leaders")]

The USC Spirit Leaders are responsible for leading stadium wide chants and increasing crowd participation at all Trojan athletic events, including football and basketball games.[[196]](#cite_note-ABOUT_«_USC_Spirit_Leaders-198)

### Student media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Student media")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/The_Southern_California_Trojan%2C_Vol._7%2C_No._11%2C_October_05%2C_1915_%28183221%29.jpg/250px-The_Southern_California_Trojan%2C_Vol._7%2C_No._11%2C_October_05%2C_1915_%28183221%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Southern_California_Trojan,_Vol._7,_No._11,_October_05,_1915_(183221).jpg)

A *The Southern California Trojan* issued in 1915

The *[Daily Trojan](/wiki/Daily_Trojan "Daily Trojan")* has been the student newspaper of USC since 1912 and is a primary source of news and information for the campus. It secured the first interview of President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") after his resignation. The publication does not receive financial aid from the university and instead runs entirely on advertisement revenue. Published from Monday to Friday during the fall and spring semesters, the newspaper turns into the Summer Trojan during the summer term and publishes once a week. It is the paper of record on campus.

[KXSC (FM)](/wiki/KXSC_(FM) "KXSC (FM)") is a University of Southern California-owned, [public radio station](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") based in [Sunnyvale, California](/wiki/Sunnyvale,_California "Sunnyvale, California"), serving the San Francisco Bay Area. KXSC-AM, previously known as KSCR, is the university's [student-run](/wiki/College_radio "College radio") station, which is managed entirely by an unpaid staff of nearly 200 undergraduate and graduate volunteers. The station gives students hands-on experience in a variety of music industry and broadcast-related positions, including live event promotion, social media management, radio production, and [audio engineering](/wiki/Audio_engineering "Audio engineering"). Besides providing nearly 24 hours of daily live programming, the station also hosts live events, bringing local and touring bands to campus. The station's annual KXSC Fest, which began in 2009, has played host to performers such as [Nosaj Thing](/wiki/Nosaj_Thing "Nosaj Thing"),[[199]](#cite_note-201) [Muna (band)](/wiki/Muna_(band) "Muna (band)"),[[200]](#cite_note-202) [Mika Miko](/wiki/Mika_Miko "Mika Miko"),[[201]](#cite_note-203) [Dan Deacon](/wiki/Dan_Deacon "Dan Deacon"),[[202]](#cite_note-204) [Thee Oh Sees](/wiki/Thee_Oh_Sees "Thee Oh Sees"),[[203]](#cite_note-205) and [Flying Lotus](/wiki/Flying_Lotus "Flying Lotus").[[204]](#cite_note-206)

KXSC traces its roots to the original [KUSC](/wiki/KUSC "KUSC"), which was operated by students starting in 1946. When KUSC transitioned to classical programming and moved off-campus in the mid-1970s, a group of students interested in having a student-run radio station founded KSCR in 1975.[[205]](#cite_note-207) KSCR was broadcast at 1560AM out of a student in the Hancock Foundation Building. In 1984, the university authorized a grant to move KSCR to a new location in Marks Hall. In 2010, KSCR adopted the call letters KXSC in order to be eligible to obtain a new FM license from the FCC, as well as to mark the station's move to a brand-new facility in the basement of the Ronald Tutor Campus Center.[[206]](#cite_note-208)

*[Trojan Vision](/wiki/Trojan_Vision "Trojan Vision")* (often abbreviated as *TV8*) is the [student television station](/wiki/Student_television_station "Student television station") at USC. TV8 was established in 1997 by the [Annenberg School for Communication](/wiki/USC_Annenberg_School_for_Communication_and_Journalism "USC Annenberg School for Communication and Journalism"), but it is now a part of the School of Cinematic Arts. Trojan Vision broadcasts 24/7 from the Robert Zemeckis Center for Digital Arts to the University Park Campus on Channel 8.1 and online through their website. Programming is also made available to the greater Los Angeles community on local channel LA36. In addition to a selection of regularly airing shows of many genres, Trojan Vision also broadcasts the shows *Platforum*, a round-table debate show; *[Annenberg TV News](/wiki/Annenberg_TV_News "Annenberg TV News")*, a [news program](/wiki/News_program "News program"); and *CU@USC*, an interview program.[[207]](#cite_note-209)

*El Rodeo* is USC's student-run [yearbook](/wiki/Yearbook "Yearbook"). One of the oldest student traditions at the university, the yearbook's first edition was released in 1889 and was originally called *The Sybil*. The name was changed to *El Rodeo* in 1899 to reflect the cowboy-themed events students threw to advertise the yearbook as a "roundup" of the year's events. It was long packaged with the Student Activity Card, which gave students access to all home sports games. Since the card was dissolved in 2007, the yearbook has been sold as a stand-alone item.[[208]](#cite_note-210)

### Fraternities and sororities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Fraternities and sororities")]

[Fraternities and sororities](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities") have had a long history on the campus. Centered on a portion of West 28th Street known as "The Row", between [Figueroa Street](/wiki/Figueroa_Street "Figueroa Street") and Hoover Street just north of campus, USC's Greek system began soon after the school's founding when [Kappa Alpha Theta](/wiki/Kappa_Alpha_Theta "Kappa Alpha Theta") founded a chapter in 1887. Today, the university sponsors four fraternities and ten sororities in the [Interfraternity Conference](/wiki/Interfraternity_Conference "Interfraternity Conference") (IFC) and [Panhellenic Conference](/wiki/National_Panhellenic_Conference "National Panhellenic Conference") (PHC), respectively.[[209]](#cite_note-211) In 2022, eleven fraternities disaffiliated from the university to form the University Park Interfraternity Council (UPIFC).[[210]](#cite_note-212)

Outside the Panhellenic and Interfraternity conferences, the Greek community at USC is very diverse, boasting the Multicultural, Asian, Inter-Fraternity (composed of professional fraternities), and the [National Pan-Hellenic](/wiki/National_Pan-Hellenic_Council "National Pan-Hellenic Council") (historically Black) Councils. Organizations governed by these councils include chapters of some of the oldest Latino and Black Greek organizations in the country, while also including established professional business, engineering, and pre-law fraternities, and other multiculturally based groups.

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### Title IX violations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Title IX violations")]

In the mid-2010s, USC was embroiled in numerous controversies and scandals. On May 1, 2014, USC was named as one of many higher-education institutions under investigation by the Office of Civil Rights for potential [Title IX](/wiki/Title_IX "Title IX") violations by [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s [White House Task Force to Protect Students from Sexual Assault](/wiki/White_House_Task_Force_to_Protect_Students_from_Sexual_Assault "White House Task Force to Protect Students from Sexual Assault").[[211]](#cite_note-213) USC is also under a concurrent Title IX investigation for potential anti-male bias in disciplinary proceedings, as well as denial of counseling resources to male students.[[212]](#cite_note-214) In 2018, USC was ordered to pay $111,965 in legal fees to a male student accused of rape after the Title IX investigation run by Gretchen Means Gaspari was deemed unfair.[[213]](#cite_note-215) In 2020, USC was penalized for its faulty Title IX processes by the U.S. Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights.[[214]](#cite_note-216)

### George Tyndall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: George Tyndall")]

The following year, the *Los Angeles Times* broke another story about USC focusing on [George Tyndall](/wiki/George_Tyndall "George Tyndall"), a gynecologist accused of abusing 52 patients at USC. The reports span from 1990 to 2016, and include using racist and sexual language, conducting exams without gloves, and taking pictures of his patients' genitals. *[Inside Higher Ed](/wiki/Inside_Higher_Ed "Inside Higher Ed")* noted that "other incidents in which the university is perceived to have failed to act on misconduct by powerful officials" have occurred,[[215]](#cite_note-217) when it reported that the university's president, [C. L. Max Nikias](/wiki/C._L._Max_Nikias "C. L. Max Nikias"), was resigning. Tyndall was fired in 2017 after reaching a settlement with the university. By June 1, 2018, 401 people had contacted a special hotline to receive complaints about the doctor.[[216]](#cite_note-218) On October 18, nearly 100 women were reported to have filed new lawsuits against the university, bringing the number of accusations up to over 500 current and former students.[[217]](#cite_note-219) A series of settlements to the victims totalled to over $1.1 billion, the largest sexual abuse settlement of any university.[[218]](#cite_note-220)

### Keck School of Medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Keck School of Medicine")]

In 2016, [Carmen Puliafito](/wiki/Carmen_Puliafito "Carmen Puliafito") resigned as dean of the Keck School of Medicine. In 2017, the Los Angeles Times revealed that Puliafito had engaged in parties with young recreational drug users and prostitutes, including at the Keck School's offices, with harm to at least one user. His resignation had occurred shortly after a police investigation of those activities. In 2018, Dennis Kelly resigned as men's health physician at USC after almost twenty years. The following year, he was accused by six male graduate students of inappropriate conduct.[[219]](#cite_note-221) By 2020, 49 accusations of misconduct had been made against Kelly, all by gay or bisexual students and former students.[[220]](#cite_note-222)

### Implication in Varsity Blues scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Implication in Varsity Blues scandal")]

USC was one of several universities involved in the [2019 college admissions bribery scandal](/wiki/2019_college_admissions_bribery_scandal "2019 college admissions bribery scandal").[[221]](#cite_note-223)[[222]](#cite_note-224) On March 12, 2019, three coaches and one athletic director were charged with accepting bribes from wealthy families for fraudulently facilitating their children's admission to USC. Among the twelve university personnel charged for their involvement in the scandal nationwide, four were associated with USC.[[223]](#cite_note-225) Following its involvement in the admissions scandal, the university was derided by its old nickname: the "University of Spoiled Children", a nickname it had previously worked hard to shed.[[224]](#cite_note-226)[[225]](#cite_note-227)[[226]](#cite_note-228)

### Preferential treatment of Qatari royal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Preferential treatment of Qatari royal")]

A 2020 investigative report by the *Los Angeles Times* revealed that USC granted a bachelor's and master's degree to [Khalifa bin Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani](/wiki/Khalifa_bin_Hamad_bin_Khalifa_Al_Thani "Khalifa bin Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani"), a Qatari royal, while allowing him to avoid rules and procedures that apply to other students. He was accepted to USC as a transfer student from [Los Angeles Mission College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Mission_College "Los Angeles Mission College") after his mother, [Moza bint Nasser](/wiki/Moza_bint_Nasser "Moza bint Nasser"), met USC president Max Nikias in 2012 in Los Angeles, California, at the behest of USC trustee [Tom Barrack](/wiki/Tom_Barrack "Tom Barrack"). An adjunct professor said that Al Thani's handlers delivered a final paper in a bag that also contained a [Rolex](/wiki/Rolex "Rolex") watch, which the adjunct returned.[[227]](#cite_note-:022-229)

### Rankings data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Rankings data")]

In December 2022, three former students in an online graduate program offered by the [Rossier School of Education](/wiki/USC_Rossier_School_of_Education "USC Rossier School of Education") and administered by educational technology company [2U](/wiki/2U_(company) "2U (company)") filed a class action lawsuit for the school's filing faulty data with *U.S. News & World Report* in an effort to boost rankings.[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231)[[230]](#cite_note-232) Rossier removed itself from the rankings in early 2022.[[231]](#cite_note-233)[[232]](#cite_note-234)

### 2024 student protests regarding the Gaza War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: 2024 student protests regarding the Gaza War")]

See also: [List of pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses in the United States in 2024](/wiki/List_of_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses_in_the_United_States_in_2024 "List of pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses in the United States in 2024")

In April 2024, the USC administration under President Carol Folt was met with widespread criticism following the decision to restrict valedictorian Asna Tabassum from speaking during that year's commencement ceremony.[[233]](#cite_note-235)[[234]](#cite_note-236) USC cited safety concerns stemming from Tabassum's pro-Palestinian viewpoints. Following several on-campus protests, statements from dozens of human rights organizations and national coverage, USC further restricted all external speakers from speaking at that year's main commencement.[[235]](#cite_note-237)

The following week, USC became embroiled in additional controversy following a [pro-Palestinian protest](/wiki/Gaza_war_protests "Gaza war protests") against the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"), beginning at 4:30 A.M. on April 24, 2024, which took place in Alumni Park, a central plaza of campus. The student organizers wrote in a release that, among other demands, the campus occupation would not end until it saw financial transparency of USC's endowments and investments, an [academic boycott of Israel](/wiki/Academic_boycott_of_Israel "Academic boycott of Israel"), and protection of [free speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech_in_the_United_States "Freedom of speech in the United States").[[236]](#cite_note-238) After involved students refused to disperse despite intervention from campus police, the university brought the Los Angeles Police Department in to remove protestors. The situation escalated to involve the complete closure of all campus facilities, the campus itself, and the arrests of 93 protestors.[[237]](#cite_note-239)

On April 25, 2024, Folt announced that the main stage commencement ceremony in May would not take place.[[238]](#cite_note-240) The actions of USC's administration towards student protestors further prompted the withdrawal of two high-profile speakers scheduled to address the USC Rossier School of Education, which was announced in an open letter to the administration on the website [Literary Hub](/wiki/Literary_Hub "Literary Hub"), as well as to USC officials.[[239]](#cite_note-241)[[240]](#cite_note-242)

In February 2025, [Leo Terrell](/wiki/Leo_Terrell "Leo Terrell"), the head of the Trump administration's [Task Force to Combat Antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_the_United_States#Efforts_to_combat_antisemitism "Antisemitism in the United States"), announced that he would investigate USC as part of the Department of Justice's broader investigation into [antisemitism on college campuses](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_US_higher_education "Antisemitism in US higher education").[[241]](#cite_note-243)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Notable people")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Southern California people](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Southern_California_people "List of University of Southern California people").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Neil_Armstrong_pose.jpg/250px-Neil_Armstrong_pose.jpg)](/wiki/File:Neil_Armstrong_pose.jpg)

Neil Armstrong completed his Master of Science degree in aerospace engineering at USC in 1970.[[242]](#cite_note-244)

USC has graduated more alumni who have gone on to win [Academy](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") and [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") than any other institution, largely due to the [School of Cinematic Arts](/wiki/USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts "USC School of Cinematic Arts"),[[243]](#cite_note-blog.studysoup.com-245)[[244]](#cite_note-cinema.usc.edu-246) and has conferred degrees upon [29 living billionaires](/wiki/List_of_universities_by_number_of_billionaire_alumni "List of universities by number of billionaire alumni").[[245]](#cite_note-247) USC has had 5 [Nobel Laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Laureates "Nobel Laureates") on staff,[[246]](#cite_note-248) and its affiliates include eleven [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholars "Rhodes Scholars"),[[247]](#cite_note-249)[[248]](#cite_note-250) twelve [Marshall Scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholarship "Marshall Scholarship"),[[249]](#cite_note-251) six [MacArthur Fellows](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows "MacArthur Fellows"),[[250]](#cite_note-252) 181 [Fulbright Scholars](/wiki/Fulbright_Scholars "Fulbright Scholars"),[[251]](#cite_note-253) and one [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") winner.[[252]](#cite_note-254)

Notable alumni also include [Neil Armstrong](/wiki/Neil_Armstrong "Neil Armstrong"), the first person to walk on the Moon; [Charles Bolden](/wiki/Charles_Bolden "Charles Bolden"), the former director of NASA and a former astronaut; [O. J. Simpson](/wiki/O._J._Simpson "O. J. Simpson"), American football player, actor, and accused murderer; [Kenneth J. Szalai](/wiki/Kenneth_J._Szalai "Kenneth J. Szalai"), former NASA Dryden director; [Byron Allen](/wiki/Byron_Allen "Byron Allen"), founder, chairman and CEO of [Allen Media Group](/wiki/Allen_Media_Group "Allen Media Group"); [Lillian Copeland](/wiki/Lillian_Copeland "Lillian Copeland"), Olympic champion in the discus throw; [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas"), creator of *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* and *[Indiana Jones](/wiki/Indiana_Jones "Indiana Jones")*; [Andrew Viterbi](/wiki/Andrew_Viterbi "Andrew Viterbi"), co-founder of [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm") and inventor of the [Viterbi algorithm](/wiki/Viterbi_algorithm "Viterbi algorithm"); [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") winner [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne"); [Dexter Holland](/wiki/Dexter_Holland "Dexter Holland"), co-founder, lead singer and guitarist of [The Offspring](/wiki/The_Offspring "The Offspring"), actor and comedian [Will Ferrell](/wiki/Will_Ferrell "Will Ferrell"); [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award")-winning actor [John Ritter](/wiki/John_Ritter "John Ritter"); [Pritzker Prize](/wiki/Pritzker_Prize "Pritzker Prize")-winning architect [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry"); Hall of Fame football player [Ron Mix](/wiki/Ron_Mix "Ron Mix"); longtime [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") owners [Jerry Buss](/wiki/Jerry_Buss "Jerry Buss"), as well as his daughter and current Lakers owner [Jeanie](/wiki/Jeanie_Buss "Jeanie Buss"); [recycling symbol](/wiki/Recycling_symbol "Recycling symbol") designer [Gary Anderson](/wiki/Gary_Anderson_(designer) "Gary Anderson (designer)"); entertainer [Larry Harmon](/wiki/Larry_Harmon "Larry Harmon"), better known as his alter ego, [Bozo the Clown](/wiki/Bozo_the_Clown "Bozo the Clown"); former [U.S. Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") [Warren Christopher](/wiki/Warren_Christopher "Warren Christopher"); deposed [Egyptian president](/wiki/President_of_Egypt "President of Egypt") [Mohamed Morsi](/wiki/Mohamed_Morsi "Mohamed Morsi"); actress [America Ferrera](/wiki/America_Ferrera "America Ferrera"); journalist [Julie Chen Moonves](/wiki/Julie_Chen_Moonves "Julie Chen Moonves"); the late [MythBusters](/wiki/MythBusters "MythBusters") host [Grant Imahara](/wiki/Grant_Imahara "Grant Imahara");[[253]](#cite_note-255) former prime minister of Jordan [Fayez Tarawneh](/wiki/Fayez_Tarawneh "Fayez Tarawneh"); Taiwanese actress and singer [Michelle Chen](/wiki/Michelle_Chen "Michelle Chen"); American influencer [Jake Shane](/wiki/Jake_Shane "Jake Shane"); and [Hwang Dong-hyuk](/wiki/Hwang_Dong-hyuk "Hwang Dong-hyuk"), South Korean director of Netflix's most watched TV show, Squid Game.

## In media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: In media")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Patsy_and_Forrest_Shumway_Fountain%2C_USC.jpg/250px-Patsy_and_Forrest_Shumway_Fountain%2C_USC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patsy_and_Forrest_Shumway_Fountain,_USC.jpg)

Fountain at central campus crossroads, Childs Way at Trousdale Parkway, with Allan Hancock Foundation building (1941) in background.

Because of USC's proximity to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_California "Hollywood, California"), close ties between the School of Cinematic Arts and entertainment industry, and the architecture on campus, the university has been used in numerous movies, television series, commercials, and music videos. USC is frequently used by filmmakers, standing in for numerous other universities. According to IMDb, USC's campus has been featured in at least 180 film and television titles.[[254]](#cite_note-Most-256)

Movies filmed at USC include *[Forrest Gump](/wiki/Forrest_Gump "Forrest Gump")*, *[Legally Blonde](/wiki/Legally_Blonde "Legally Blonde")*, *[Road Trip](/wiki/Road_Trip_(2000_film) "Road Trip (2000 film)")*, *[The Girl Next Door](/wiki/The_Girl_Next_Door_(2004_film) "The Girl Next Door (2004 film)")*, *[Harold & Kumar Go to White Castle](/wiki/Harold_%26_Kumar_Go_to_White_Castle "Harold & Kumar Go to White Castle")*, *[Love & Basketball](/wiki/Love_%26_Basketball "Love & Basketball")*, *[Blue Chips](/wiki/Blue_Chips "Blue Chips")*, *[Ghostbusters](/wiki/Ghostbusters "Ghostbusters")*, *[Live Free or Die Hard](/wiki/Live_Free_or_Die_Hard "Live Free or Die Hard")*, *[House Party 2](/wiki/House_Party_2 "House Party 2")*, *[The Number 23](/wiki/The_Number_23 "The Number 23")*, *[The Social Network](/wiki/The_Social_Network "The Social Network")* and *[The Graduate](/wiki/The_Graduate "The Graduate")*.[[255]](#cite_note-257) Television series that have used the USC campus include *[Brooklyn Nine-Nine](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nine-Nine "Brooklyn Nine-Nine")*, *[How to Get Away With Murder](/wiki/How_to_Get_Away_With_Murder "How to Get Away With Murder")*, *[Cold Case](/wiki/Cold_Case_(TV_series) "Cold Case (TV series)")*, *[Entourage](/wiki/Entourage_(U.S._TV_series) "Entourage (U.S. TV series)")*, *[24](/wiki/24_(TV_series) "24 (TV series)")*, *[The O.C.](/wiki/The_O.C. "The O.C.")*, *[Beverly Hills, 90210](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_90210 "Beverly Hills, 90210")*, *[Moesha](/wiki/Moesha "Moesha")*, *[Saved by the Bell: The College Years](/wiki/Saved_by_the_Bell:_The_College_Years "Saved by the Bell: The College Years")*, *[The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air](/wiki/The_Fresh_Prince_of_Bel-Air "The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air")*, *[House M.D.](/wiki/House_(TV_series) "House (TV series)")*, *[CSI: NY](/wiki/CSI:_NY "CSI: NY")*, *[Undeclared](/wiki/Undeclared "Undeclared")*, *[The West Wing](/wiki/The_West_Wing "The West Wing")*, *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")*, *[The Office](/wiki/The_Office_(U.S._TV_series) "The Office (U.S. TV series)")*, *[Monk](/wiki/Monk_(TV_series) "Monk (TV series)")*, *[The United States of Tara](/wiki/The_United_States_of_Tara "The United States of Tara")*, *[Gilmore Girls](/wiki/Gilmore_Girls "Gilmore Girls")*, *[Scrubs](/wiki/Scrubs_(TV_series) "Scrubs (TV series)")*, and *[The Roommate](/wiki/The_Roommate "The Roommate")*.[[256]](#cite_note-258)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Southern_California&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Notes")]

**a.** [**^**](#ref_Anone) The alternative name "Southern Cal" frequently appears in sports-related news articles to distinguish it from the [University of South Carolina](/wiki/University_of_South_Carolina "University of South Carolina"), which also uses the "USC" acronym. USC discourages use of "Southern Cal" out of concern the name might suggest a foundational association to the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley") (commonly known as "Cal" in the athletics context), even though the two institutions have no affiliation other than their longtime (but not current) Pac-12 membership and the fact that they are in the same state. For several years, USC's [media guides](/wiki/Media_guide "Media guide") contained the following request: "Note to the media: In editorial references to athletic teams of the University of Southern California, the following are preferred: USC, Southern California, So. California, Troy and Trojans for men's or women's teams, and Women of Troy for women's teams. The use of 'Southern Cal' on licensed apparel and merchandise is limited in scope and necessary to protect federal [trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") rights."[[257]](#cite_note-notsoutherncal-259)

**b.** [**^**](#ref_Bnone) Specifically [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"); Hong Kong, China; [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"); [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"), [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China"); [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"); and Tokyo, Japan. [USC International Offices](https://web.archive.org/web/20060520205150/http://www.usc.edu/dept/International_Offices/)

**c.** [**^**](#ref_Cnone) The NCAA does not conduct a championship for Football Bowl Subdivision football.

**d.** [**^**](#ref_Dnone) The precise colors can be found on the [USC Graphic Identity Program website](https://web.archive.org/web/20071229023600/http://www.usc.edu/identity/print/colors_and_typefaces/): the correct [Pantone](/wiki/Pantone "Pantone") color for USC Cardinal is PMS 201C and USC Gold is PMS 123C.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-125)** The percentage of students who received an income-based federal [Pell grant](/wiki/Pell_grant "Pell grant") intended for low-income students.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-126)** The percentage of students who are a part of the [American middle class](/wiki/American_middle_class "American middle class") at the bare minimum.

"A search for a successor to Dr. Fred D. Fagg sixth president of the University of Southern California, has begun in earnest, it was learned yesterday. Dr. Fagg, who guided the university through its veteran-swollen enrollment years after World War II, completed 10 years head lot the Trojan campus this week and retired to his new Pasadena residence with no plans for the immediate future... Dr. Fagg, 60, announced his resignation last Jan. 11, stating that he did not "possess the resources of health and energy to meet" the challenging requirements of the office. His resignation became effective last Monday and simultaneously, he acquired the title of president emeritus. The university's three vice-presidents, Earl Bolton, Robert Fisher and Albert Raubenheimer, will direct affairs of SC until a successor is named."
